title: Jātakamālā
author: Āryaśūra
genre: kavya / jataka (campū-style birth-story collection in mixed verse and prose)
date_estimate: c. 3rd-4th c. CE (sometimes placed earlier; debated)
summary: The 'Garland of Birth-Stories' retells in polished classical Sanskrit thirty-four jatakas, edifying tales of the previous lives of the Buddha-to-be (Bodhisattva), each illustrating a moral perfection (pāramitā) such as generosity, patience, and self-sacrifice. The stories alternate ornate verse with elegant prose and conclude with a doctrinal or ethical moral.
history: A celebrated work of Buddhist kavya whose author Āryaśūra is sometimes (uncertainly) identified with Aśvaghoṣa; its scenes parallel paintings at Ajanta, indicating wide influence. The Sanskrit editio princeps appeared in 1891 as the first volume of the Harvard Oriental Series.

title: Jātakamālā
author: Āryaśūra
genre: kavya / jataka (mixed verse and prose birth-story collection)
summary: A second catalogue record of Āryaśūra's 'Garland of Birth-Stories,' the same canonical collection of thirty-four jatakas recounting the Bodhisattva's former lives, each demonstrating a perfection (pāramitā) through self-sacrifice and virtue in ornate Sanskrit prose-and-verse.
history: Identical work to the other Āryaśūra Jātakamālā entry in this batch; a foundational Buddhist Sanskrit kavya, edited in the Harvard Oriental Series and illustrated in the Ajanta murals.

title: Bodhisattvāvadānakalpalatā
author: Kṣemendra (with the final avadāna added by his son Somendra)
tradition: Buddhist (Mahāyāna), composed by a Kashmiri Hindu polymath
summary: A large verse collection of 108 avadanas recounting the meritorious past-life deeds of the Buddha and bodhisattvas, presented as a 'wish-fulfilling creeper' (kalpalatā) of edifying legends emphasizing the bodhisattva ideal of self-sacrifice. Kṣemendra composed the first 107 tales; his son Somendra added the concluding Jīmūtavāhanāvadāna and the frame verses.
history: Written by the prolific 11th-century Kashmiri poet and satirist Kṣemendra (c. 990-c. 1070), it became extremely influential in Tibet, where it was translated and richly illustrated. Sanskrit text survives and was edited from Nepalese and Tibetan sources.

title: Jātakamālā [Ajātaśatrvavadāna]
author: Gopadatta
genre: avadana / jataka (mixed verse and prose narrative)
summary: Gopadatta's Jātakamālā is a separate 'Garland of Birth-Stories' modeled on Āryaśūra's, retelling some thirty-four legends of the Buddha's previous lives in a mixture of verse and prose. The Ajātaśatrvāvadāna is one of its constituent legends, treating the famous story of King Ajātaśatru, the patricide of Magadha, and his ultimate redemption.
history: Gopadatta lived in the seventh or eighth century; only about half of his Garland has been recovered from later Sanskrit story-anthologies. The Ajātaśatrvāvadāna was attributed to Gopadatta by Michael Hahn, who reconstructed and edited much of the corpus.

title: Jātakastava
author: Jñānayaśas
genre: stotra (devotional hymn in praise of the Buddha's former births)
summary: A religious poem in praise of the Buddha glorifying his self-sacrificing deeds across his previous births (jatakas). It is built on an iterative hymnic structure with introductory and concluding stanzas, allusively recalling famous jataka episodes as objects of veneration rather than narrating them in full.
history: The Sanskrit Jātakastava of Jñānayaśas survives with an interlinear Tibetan gloss and belongs to the same hymnographic genre as the better-known Khotanese Jātakastava, though it is a distinct, more elaborate composition. It was studied and edited by D. R. Shackleton Bailey (1939, 1954).

title: Ratnāvalī [Ratnamālā]
author: Nāgārjuna
genre: philosophical treatise / didactic epistle (rajaparikatha, advice to a king)
summary: The 'Precious Garland' (also Ratnāvalī rājaparikathā) is a verse work of advice addressed to a king that interweaves Madhyamaka philosophy (emptiness, the two truths, dependent origination) with ethical, political, and religious counsel on righteous governance and the bodhisattva path. It uniquely combines subtle metaphysics with worldly statecraft.
history: Attributed to Nāgārjuna, founder of the Madhyamaka school, whose dates and even number remain debated (1st-3rd c. CE). It is one of his most accessible works and survives partly in Sanskrit and fully in Tibetan and Chinese; it is counted among his epistolary/advisory writings alongside the Suhṛllekha.

title: Bhavasaṃkrāntiparikathā
author: Nāgārjuna (attributed)
date_estimate: c. 2nd-3rd c. CE (with Nāgārjuna; attribution and date uncertain)
summary: A short Madhyamaka treatise on 'transmigration of existence' (bhavasaṃkrānti) that addresses how rebirth and the passage from one existence to another can occur in the absence of any permanent self or transferable entity, explaining karmic continuity in terms of emptiness and dependent origination.
history: Attributed to Nāgārjuna and associated with the corpus of Madhyamaka logical works; the Bhavasaṃkrānti (sūtra and śāstra) was edited by N. A. Sastri (Adyar Library, 1938). Its authorship and date, like several minor works ascribed to Nāgārjuna, are debated.

title: Śiṣyalekha (alternate)
author: Candragomin
date_estimate: c. 5th-7th c. CE (Candragomin variously dated; this record an alternate version of the text)
summary: An alternate/variant version of Candragomin's 'Letter to a Disciple,' an ornate verse epistle expounding Buddhist doctrine, the sufferings of saṃsāra, and the bodhisattva path. It was addressed to a disciple (traditionally Ratnakīrti) to recall him from worldly attachment back to the religious life.
history: Candragomin, a celebrated Buddhist grammarian and lay poet, composed the Śiṣyalekha, regarded as the most refined of the surviving Buddhist epistles. This entry is a second recension/version of the text; it was edited by I. P. Minaev (1889) and M. Hahn (1998).

title: Śiṣyalekha
author: Candragomin
summary: The 'Letter to a Disciple' is a short, highly ornate kavya in epistolary form that sets out Buddhist teaching on impermanence, the perils of saṃsāra, the rarity of the human birth, and the bodhisattva's compassionate path. It is framed as an exhortation to a disciple who had abandoned his vows for a love affair.
history: Composed by the lay scholar and grammarian Candragomin (dated variously 5th-7th c. CE), it is considered the most refined extant Buddhist epistle. The Sanskrit survives (Cambridge and other manuscripts) and was edited by Minaev and later Michael Hahn.

title: Daṇḍikathā
author: anonymous/traditional
summary: Apparently a short Buddhist narrative tale (kathā); the title, meaning roughly 'the story of the staff/of Daṇḍin,' suggests an edifying or avadāna-type story, possibly a moral or karmic narrative in the manner of Buddhist story collections. The specific content could not be confidently identified.
history: A minor and poorly attested Buddhist Sanskrit text known mainly as a catalogue/corpus entry; its author, date, and precise contents are obscure and were not verifiable from the sources consulted.

title: Vimalaratnalekha
author: Atiśa Dīpaṅkara Śrījñāna
genre: lekha (didactic epistle of moral counsel to a king)
summary: The 'Spotless Jewel Letter' is an epistle of ethical and religious guidance addressed to the Pāla king Nayapāla of Magadha, offering a collection of moral maxims rooted in bodhisattva ideals and Buddhist moral philosophy for the conduct of a devout ruler.
history: Composed by Atiśa Dīpaṅkara Śrījñāna, the influential 11th-century master who later revitalized Buddhism in Tibet, at the request of King Nayapāla. The Sanskrit original is lost; it survives in Tibetan and has been reconstructed into Sanskrit with translation by modern scholars.

title: Ratnāvalī [Ratnamālā] 1
author: Nāgārjuna
summary: Chapter/section 1 of Nāgārjuna's 'Precious Garland,' the verse advice-to-a-king that blends Madhyamaka philosophy of emptiness with ethical and political counsel. The opening portion lays out the foundations of higher status (abhyudaya) and definite goodness (niḥśreyasa) and the basics of the path.
history: Part of the same Ratnāvalī rājaparikathā attributed to Nāgārjuna; this record represents one division of the work as transmitted. The text survives partly in Sanskrit and fully in Tibetan and Chinese.

title: Ratnāvalī [Ratnamālā] 2
author: Nāgārjuna
summary: Chapter/section 2 of Nāgārjuna's 'Precious Garland,' continuing the integrated exposition of Madhyamaka emptiness and the bodhisattva path together with practical counsel to the king on virtuous rule and merit.
history: A division of the Ratnāvalī rājaparikathā ascribed to Nāgārjuna, transmitted in Sanskrit fragments and in complete Tibetan and Chinese versions.

title: Ratnāvalī [Ratnamālā] 3
author: Nāgārjuna
summary: A further chapter/section of Nāgārjuna's 'Precious Garland,' developing the union of Madhyamaka philosophy (emptiness, the bodhisattva's accumulation of merit and wisdom) with ethical guidance addressed to a king. (Note: the work_id suffix '_4' alongside the displayed '3' suggests a numbering offset in the corpus.)
history: Another transmitted division of Nāgārjuna's Ratnāvalī rājaparikathā, preserved in Sanskrit fragments and in Tibetan and Chinese translation.

title: Subhāṣitaratnakaraṇḍakathā
author: attributed to Āryaśūra (also given as Śūra/Aśvaghoṣa; uncertain)
summary: The 'Casket of Jewel-like Well-Spoken Verses' is a Buddhist didactic verse collection setting out moral and religious instruction (well-turned aphorisms, subhāṣitas) on virtue, the disadvantages of vice, and the conduct conducive to the Buddhist path. It is a gnomic/edifying work distinct from Vidyākara's later secular anthology of similar name.
history: Traditionally ascribed to Āryaśūra (the title sometimes circulates under Śūra or Aśvaghoṣa), the attribution and date are uncertain. It survives in Sanskrit and Tibetan and belongs to the early Buddhist subhāṣita tradition.

title: Hetubinduṭīkā
author: Arcaṭa (Bhaṭṭa Arcaṭa)
summary: A detailed commentary on Dharmakīrti's Hetubindu ('Drop of Reason'), a short treatise on the logical reason/probans (hetu) in Buddhist inference. Arcaṭa expounds the theory of valid inference, the triple-character of the logical reason (trairūpya), and topics such as the inference of momentariness (kṣaṇikatvānumāna).
history: Composed by the Buddhist logician Bhaṭṭa Arcaṭa (8th c.), it is a key link in the post-Dharmakīrti pramāṇa tradition. It was transmitted with the later sub-commentary Āloka of Durveka Miśra and edited together at Baroda in 1949.

title: Hetubinduṭīkā
author: Arcaṭa (Bhaṭṭa Arcaṭa)
summary: A second catalogue record of Arcaṭa's commentary on Dharmakīrti's Hetubindu, explicating the Buddhist theory of the logical reason (hetu), the three conditions of a valid probans (trairūpya), and associated epistemological problems.
history: Same work as the other Hetubinduṭīkā entry in this batch; a major eighth-century commentary in the Buddhist epistemological tradition, edited with Durveka Miśra's sub-commentary Āloka (Baroda, 1949).

title: Ālambanaparīkṣā
author: Dignāga
summary: The 'Investigation of the Object/Percept' is a very short verse treatise (about eight kārikās) arguing that the object of perception cannot be an external atom or aggregate of atoms. Instead, what is cognized and what causes cognition is only an internal mental form, thereby supporting Yogācāra idealism.
history: A foundational work of Dignāga, the founder of the Buddhist epistemological (pramāṇa) tradition. The Sanskrit survives only in fragments; the text is preserved in Tibetan and in several Chinese translations (e.g., by Paramārtha and Xuanzang) and generated extensive commentary.

title: Ālambanaparīkṣāvṛtti
author: Dignāga
summary: Dignāga's own prose auto-commentary on his Ālambanaparīkṣā, unpacking the verses' arguments that the perceptual object cannot be external (neither atoms nor their aggregates can satisfy the requirements of an object-support, ālambana) and that the genuine object is an internal cognitive form, in support of mind-only (Yogācāra) idealism.
history: The vṛtti is transmitted together with the kārikās; like the root text it survives only in Sanskrit fragments and is preserved in Tibetan (Derge 4206) and Chinese. It was the focus of refutations by realist opponents such as Śubhagupta.

title: Vajrasūcī
author: Aśvaghoṣa (attribution disputed)
date_estimate: c. 2nd c. CE if by Aśvaghoṣa; attribution and date contested
summary: The 'Diamond Needle' is a short polemical tract that mounts a vigorous attack on the brahminical caste system, arguing that there is in reality only one undifferentiated varṇa and that birth does not confer the qualities claimed for brahmanhood. It refutes the brahmins using the authority of their own scriptures.
history: Traditionally ascribed to Aśvaghoṣa (c. 80-150 CE), the attribution is seriously contested (the maṅgala invokes Mañjuśrī, supporting a Buddhist origin, but scholars including Olivelle doubt Aśvaghoṣa's authorship). A closely related Brahmanical recension circulates as the Vajrasūcikā Upaniṣad.
